title: Credential Compromise: Fraudulent CNP Channel Shift on Card-Present Cardholder
narrative: Fraudster exploits stolen card credentials to conduct card-not-present transactions on a card with strong historical card-present usage patterns. The card's legitimate owner primarily uses chip-and-PIN or contactless payment at physical retail locations, establishing a clean baseline of card-present activity. The compromised credentials enable online purchases that pass basic verification checks—correct CVV, valid billing address, matching email domain patterns—but the sudden injection of CNP transactions creates a detectable channel-mix anomaly. Card-level aggregation features reveal an unusual CNP-to-total transaction ratio that contradicts the cardholder's established behavior. Individual transaction amounts remain within the cardholder's typical spending range to avoid behavioral red flags, but the C-counter distributions expose the channel shift. This pattern exploits the assumption that valid credentials equal valid authorization, relying on merchants not verifying channel-consistency against cardholder history.
indicators: C8 < 3 indicating very low historical card-present transaction count in recent window
C1 > 12 with C8/C1 ratio < 0.15, showing CNP transactions dominate recent card activity
C13 > 7 (p75+) revealing elevated card-email pairing count inconsistent with single-channel user
card1_amt_zscore between -1.0 and +1.5, amounts fitting cardholder's historical spending pattern
V308 or V310 > 4.2 (p85), Vesta features flagging channel-transition anomaly
D1 = 0 or D2 = 0 indicating first-time device-card pairing for CNP context
C14 > 2 (p75+) showing card used across multiple product categories in compressed timeframe
indicator_explanations: The card has minimal recent history of in-person chip or swipe transactions
Card-not-present purchases vastly outnumber card-present activity in the rolling window
Card appears with more email addresses than expected for a single cardholder
Purchase amounts align with the cardholder's normal spending to avoid triggering amount-based alerts
Risk model detects unusual payment-channel shift inconsistent with cardholder profile
This device has never processed a transaction with this card number before
Card purchasing activity spans more product types than the cardholder's established preferences
summary: Stolen card credentials used for CNP transactions on a card with predominantly card-present history, creating channel-mix inconsistency. Amounts stay in-pattern but aggregated counters expose the unauthorized CNP shift.
reasoning: Legitimate cardholders maintain consistent payment-channel preferences; a sudden shift from card-present to card-not-present with new device pairings and elevated email diversity indicates credential theft rather than changed user behavior.
